heroOverview: Stannosis is a benign pneumoconiosis caused by the inhalation and deposition of tin oxide dust in the lungs, affecting workers in tin smelting, tin ore processing, and the production of tin compounds. Tin oxide (SnO₂) is among the most radiodense of inhaled mineral particles, producing strikingly dense, miliary opacities on chest X-rays that can alarm both the patient and an unfamiliar clinician. Yet pure stannosis causes little or no pulmonary fibrosis, and lung function is almost universally preserved. The condition is significant primarily because its alarming radiology must be correctly distinguished from more serious conditions — tuberculosis, silicosis, and malignancy — and because continued exposure can be prevented with appropriate workplace controls. When tin exposure is combined with silica, a fibrogenic mixed-dust pneumoconiosis can develop.

keyStats.prevalence: Stannosis is rare; the small size of the tin processing workforce worldwide means only hundreds of cases are documented in the medical literature, predominantly from tin smelters and cassiterite miners
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly men in their 30s-60s employed in tin mining, smelting, and tin compound manufacturing industries
keyStats.avgAge: Radiological changes typically emerge after 15-25 years of exposure to tin oxide dust
keyStats.globalCases: Cases have been reported primarily from Bolivia, Malaysia, Cornwall (United Kingdom), and Australia historically; modern cases reflect remaining tin processing industries globally

definition: Stannosis (ICD-10: J63.5) is a pneumoconiosis resulting from the accumulation of tin oxide (cassiterite, SnO₂) or metallic tin dust particles in the pulmonary interstitium and alveolar macrophages. The condition falls under pulmonology and occupational medicine. Tin oxide is highly radio-opaque — approximately three times the density of calcium — which explains the striking and diagnostically misleading chest X-ray appearances. Macrophages in the alveoli engulf tin particles but, unlike the situation with silica or asbestos, tin oxide does not induce significant collagen formation or disrupt macrophage viability; the cellular response is essentially accumulative rather than destructive. This distinction makes pure stannosis a 'benign pneumoconiosis' analogous to siderosis (iron oxide) and baritosis (barium sulphate). The important caveat is that tin ore mining, particularly cassiterite mining, frequently involves co-exposure to crystalline silica from quartz veins in the ore, and the resulting mixed disease — tin-silicosis — is fibrogenic and progressive. The natural history of pure stannosis is essentially stable: particles remain in the lung indefinitely, opacities persist on imaging, but respiratory function does not decline. Accurate diagnosis requires an occupational history in the tin industry and exclusion of the several conditions that produce a similar radiological picture.

typesClassification.description: Deposition of tin oxide alone without fibrosis or functional impairment; the classic benign form producing dense miliary opacities with normal spirometry and DLCO.
Co-exposure to silica and tin in cassiterite mining or processing; produces true progressive fibrosis because silica is the active fibrogenic component; functional decline occurs over years.
Inhalation of tin chloride or other reactive tin compounds, which are irritant rather than simply accumulative, causing acute or subacute chemical airway inflammation distinct from classic stannosis.

primarySymptoms: Absence of symptoms in the majority of patients with pure stannosis — the condition is most commonly discovered incidentally during a chest X-ray performed for another reason.
Chronic dry cough developing gradually after many years of exposure, often mild and not disabling.
Mild exertional breathlessness, particularly in patients with long exposure histories or those developing mixed tin-silicosis.
Chest tightness or heaviness after prolonged heavy exposure days, often self-limiting.
Recurrent respiratory tract infections — impaired mucociliary clearance from accumulated particles increases susceptibility to bronchitis.
Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance in patients who have developed mixed dust disease or complicating emphysema from long careers in dusty trades.
Wheezing on exertion or in response to respiratory irritants, more common when welding fumes or other airway irritants accompany tin dust.
Constitutional symptoms such as fever and night sweats are not features of pure stannosis and should prompt urgent investigation for tuberculosis or malignancy.
In advanced mixed tin-silicosis: progressive breathlessness at rest, cyanosis, and signs of cor pulmonale.
earlyWarningSigns: Abnormal chest X-ray showing dense bilateral nodules in a tin industry worker on routine occupational surveillance.
Mild persistent cough that began insidiously during the years of exposure without an infectious cause.
Slightly reduced exercise tolerance noticed over several months.
DLCO at the lower limit of normal on surveillance pulmonary function tests.
emergencySigns: Haemoptysis combined with weight loss and constitutional symptoms, requiring urgent exclusion of tuberculosis or lung cancer.
Rapid deterioration of breathlessness over days to weeks, which may signal a superimposed acute infection, pulmonary embolism, or progressive massive fibrosis in mixed disease.
Oxygen saturation below 90% at rest in a known tin worker, indicating severe respiratory compromise.
New-onset chest pain with haemoptysis, requiring emergency imaging to exclude malignancy or pneumothorax.

causes.cause: Inhalation of tin oxide (SnO₂) dust during tin ore processing
causes.description: Cassiterite (tin oxide) ore is crushed, milled, and processed to extract tin metal; these activities generate fine respirable particles that deposit in the alveoli and are taken up by macrophages, accumulating over years without being cleared.

causes.cause: Tin smelter and refinery dust exposure
causes.description: Smelting tin ore and refining tin metal produce tin oxide fumes and fine particles; workers in furnace operations and casting areas accumulate significant lung burden over careers lasting 10-30 years.
Production of inorganic tin compounds for industrial use involves handling fine tin oxide powder; inadequate containment allows inhalation of respirable fractions in factory workers.

causes.cause: Co-exposure to silica in cassiterite mining
causes.description: Tin ore bodies (cassiterite) are embedded in quartz-bearing rock; drilling and blasting release both tin oxide and crystalline silica; silica activates macrophage death and fibroblast collagen synthesis, converting the benign tin deposit into fibrogenic mixed disease.

Alveolar macrophages engulf tin oxide particles efficiently but cannot transport the accumulated volume to the mucociliary escalator over a long career; particles remain in alveolar walls and peribronchiolar tissue indefinitely.

causes.cause: Working in poorly ventilated tin processing facilities
causes.description: Without effective local exhaust ventilation, airborne tin oxide concentrations in grinding, milling, or furnace areas can greatly exceed safe limits, accelerating the rate of pulmonary accumulation.

riskFactors.factor: Employment in tin mining or smelting
riskFactors.description: The primary occupational risk; cassiterite miners and tin smelter workers accumulate the highest lung burdens; risk is proportional to years worked and dust concentration.
Tin ore extraction from quartz-bearing rock is the most common route to clinically significant mixed disease; silica converts the benign accumulation into progressive fibrosis.
Radiological stannosis develops after 10-25 years of significant exposure; workers with shorter careers at low dust levels may show minimal or no changes.
Absence of local exhaust ventilation and non-use of appropriate respirators allow exposures well above occupational exposure limits; correcting these is the most effective preventive intervention.
Tobacco use independently impairs mucociliary clearance and alveolar macrophage function and adds COPD to whatever dust-related pathology is present.
Dry sweeping, absence of wet suppression at dust sources, and lack of changing facilities increase total dust burden on workers and nearby individuals.

affectedDemographics: Workers in cassiterite (tin ore) mines, particularly underground miners
Tin smelter and refinery workers exposed to tin oxide fumes
Tin compound and tin salt manufacturing workers
Foundry workers who handle tin-containing alloys such as solder and pewter
Workers in countries with active tin industries including Bolivia, Indonesia, Malaysia, China, and Australia
Maintenance workers in tin-processing plants who are intermittently exposed to accumulated dust
diagnosisOverview: Stannosis is almost always identified through occupational health surveillance rather than symptomatic presentation, because pure stannosis does not impair lung function. The diagnosis is established by correlating an occupational history of tin dust exposure with characteristic chest imaging. The chest X-ray in stannosis is distinctive: bilateral, extremely dense, small nodules that appear more radio-opaque per unit size than calcium — an appearance sometimes compared to 'snow storm' or 'metallic dust' — scattered through both lung fields with no particular lobar predominance. HRCT confirms the pattern and grades severity, showing perilymphatic micronodules with high attenuation on soft-tissue windows and helping distinguish stannosis from the pleural plaques of asbestosis and the upper-lobe nodules of silicosis. Pulmonary function tests — spirometry and DLCO — are the critical functional discriminant: in pure stannosis they are normal, while any restriction, obstruction, or DLCO impairment indicates either mixed dust disease or a complicating condition. Bronchoalveolar lavage may show tin-laden macrophages, which can be confirmed by energy-dispersive X-ray microanalysis of the particles. The principal differentials requiring active exclusion are miliary tuberculosis, miliary carcinomatosis, silicosis (with which tin-silicosis overlaps), sarcoidosis, and haemosiderosis. All workers with stannosis should be tested for latent tuberculosis, as pneumoconiosis independently impairs alveolar macrophage TB-killing capacity.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line screening for bilateral dense nodular opacities; the hyperopaque nodules of stannosis are characteristic and an experienced radiologist or ILO B-reader recognises the pattern.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard, painless chest X-ray taking under five minutes; results reviewed by a radiologist familiar with pneumoconiosis or an ILO-certified B-reader.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises nodule density, size, distribution, and detects any fibrosis, emphysema, or progressive massive fibrosis not visible on plain X-ray.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a CT scanner for a few minutes; no contrast injection is required for standard chest HRCT.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes benign pure stannosis (normal function) from mixed tin-silicosis (restrictive pattern with reduced DLCO); the single most important functional test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You breathe through a mouthpiece into a spirometer and hold your breath briefly for DLCO measurement; total test time 30-45 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies tin-containing particles in macrophages confirming the diagnosis; energy-dispersive X-ray microanalysis specifies the elemental content.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed during fibre-optic bronchoscopy under moderate sedation; lavage fluid from a lung segment is analysed under electron microscopy.

diagnosticTests.test: Tuberculin skin test or IGRA (TB blood test)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for latent tuberculosis infection, which is more common in all pneumoconiosis patients due to impaired alveolar macrophage function.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Either a small injection under the skin (Mantoux, read at 72 hours) or a standard blood draw (IGRA, results within 24 hours).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses functional capacity and detects exertional oxygen desaturation in patients with possible mixed disease or symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You walk as fast as you comfortably can for six minutes along a measured flat corridor while a clinician monitors your oxygen saturation and heart rate.

treatmentOverview: Pure stannosis requires no pharmacological treatment because it causes no fibrosis and no functional impairment. The essential management step is minimising further tin oxide inhalation, achieved either by relocating the worker away from dusty tasks or by significantly improving engineering controls and respiratory protection at the worksite. Once removed from exposure, lung function in pure stannosis remains stable indefinitely, and no progressive deterioration is expected. For workers diagnosed with mixed tin-silicosis, management parallels the approach to silicosis: removal from all silica exposure, pulmonary rehabilitation, bronchodilator therapy for any airflow obstruction following standard COPD management guidelines, and supplemental oxygen when resting SpO₂ falls to 88% or below. Corticosteroids are not standard therapy for stannosis or tin-silicosis, distinguishing these conditions from granulomatous lung diseases such as berylliosis and sarcoidosis where immunosuppression is the primary treatment. Tuberculosis screening and treatment is a priority in all pneumoconiosis patients because macrophage-mediated TB killing is impaired by accumulated particles; isoniazid preventive therapy should follow standard guidelines for latent TB. Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations are recommended. Smoking cessation is strongly encouraged and is the most impactful modifiable behaviour. Lung transplantation remains an option for end-stage mixed disease in suitable candidates. Longitudinal surveillance with annual chest imaging and pulmonary function testing allows detection of any new complication — TB, lung cancer, or progressive fibrosis in mixed disease — at the earliest opportunity, when intervention is most effective.

medicalTreatments.name: Removal from tin oxide exposure
medicalTreatments.description: The primary management intervention; relocating the worker prevents further particle accumulation. Pure stannosis stabilises after exposure ceases. Workers should be moved to roles without tin dust contact rather than simply given respiratory protection alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effectively halts further radiological progression in pure stannosis; slows but may not stop progression in established mixed tin-silicosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Bronchodilators (SABA, LABA, LAMA)
medicalTreatments.description: Used when airflow obstruction accompanies stannosis or mixed tin-silicosis; prescribed according to COPD guidelines based on severity of obstruction and symptom burden.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improve FEV1 by 12-15% in obstructed patients and reduce breathlessness scores comparably to COPD bronchodilator trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Supplemental oxygen therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Prescribed when resting SpO₂ is at or below 88% or when significant exertional desaturation limits activity; long-term oxygen therapy reduces right heart strain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality and improves quality of life in chronically hypoxaemic patients with progressive lung disease, based on landmark NOTT and MRC trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Isoniazid preventive therapy (if LTBI positive)
medicalTreatments.description: Six to nine months of isoniazid (or an alternative regimen) for workers with stannosis who test positive for latent TB infection, because pneumoconiosis significantly elevates the risk of TB reactivation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces the risk of active TB reactivation by approximately 60-90% in LTBI-positive individuals according to WHO guidelines.

medicalTreatments.name: Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination
medicalTreatments.description: All stannosis patients are recommended annual influenza vaccination and a pneumococcal vaccine (PCV and PPSV23) to reduce the risk of severe respiratory infections that can destabilise already-compromised lung function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Influenza vaccination reduces hospitalisation by approximately 35-45% in adults with chronic lung conditions; pneumococcal vaccination reduces invasive disease by 45-75%.

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for end-stage mixed tin-silicosis with severe, progressive functional impairment and no contraindications; outcomes are comparable to other end-stage non-infectious fibrotic lung diseases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year post-transplant survival for fibrotic lung disease is approximately 50-55% based on international registry data.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Supervised exercise training, breathing techniques, and patient education; improves exercise tolerance and reduces breathlessness even when the underlying lung pathology cannot be reversed; recommended for all symptomatic patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Smoking cessation programmes
alternativeTreatments.description: Pharmacotherapy (nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion) combined with behavioural counselling significantly improves quit rates; stopping smoking reduces annual lung function decline and lowers infection and cancer risk.

whySeeSpecialist: Any worker in the tin industry with abnormal chest imaging should be evaluated by a pulmonologist experienced in occupational lung diseases. The principal tasks are confirming the diagnosis of stannosis, excluding tuberculosis, malignancy, and silicosis, measuring lung function to detect any mixed disease, and arranging appropriate workplace removal and surveillance. An occupational medicine physician supports exposure assessment and compensation claims.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a pulmonologist with an interest in pneumoconiosis or occupational lung disease, ideally at a centre with access to HRCT, bronchoscopy, pulmonary function laboratories, and specialists familiar with the ILO chest X-ray classification. If silicosis or mixed disease is a possibility, oncology input and TB services should be co-located or accessible. Ask whether the team routinely performs TB screening and has a surveillance protocol for tin industry workers.
preventionStrategies: Maintain airborne tin oxide dust below the occupational exposure limit — 2 mg/m³ (total) or 1 mg/m³ (respirable fraction) in most jurisdictions — using local exhaust ventilation and wet suppression.
Provide suitable respiratory protective equipment — P100 particulate respirators or powered air-purifying respirators — for any task where engineering controls cannot achieve safe levels.
Conduct regular occupational health surveillance including chest X-ray and spirometry for all workers exposed to tin or tin-silica dust.
Eliminate dry sweeping and dry processing where possible; substitute with wet methods or automated enclosed processes to minimise fugitive dust.
Educate workers on the health risks of tin dust, the use of respiratory protection, and the importance of reporting symptoms early.
Reduce silica co-exposure in cassiterite mining by dust suppression at drilling points and using silica-safe rock blasting practices.
lifestyleModifications: Quit smoking — this single step has the largest impact on preserving remaining lung function in any pneumoconiosis.
Exercise regularly within your functional limits to maintain cardiorespiratory fitness; even walking 20-30 minutes daily improves respiratory muscle endurance.
Avoid home exposure to respiratory irritants such as aerosol sprays, chemical cleaners, and dusty environments.
Keep all scheduled surveillance appointments and report any change in symptoms, including new cough, haemoptysis, or worsening breathlessness, between appointments.
Stay up to date with respiratory vaccinations — annual influenza and pneumococcal — to reduce infection risk.

recommended: A well-balanced, nutrient-dense diet to maintain body weight and respiratory muscle strength
Adequate hydration to keep airway secretions moist and easier to clear
Foods rich in antioxidants — fruit, vegetables, whole grains — to counter the oxidative burden of chronic dust accumulation
Small, frequent meals if breathlessness limits tolerating large amounts at once
avoid: All tobacco products in any form
Excess alcohol, which impairs immune defence and worsens mucociliary clearance
High-salt processed foods if bronchodilators, diuretics, or corticosteroids are prescribed for complicating disease

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is encouraged for all stannosis patients who do not have significant functional impairment. Formal pulmonary rehabilitation is the best evidence-based programme for those with breathlessness limiting activity. Exercise in clean outdoor air at moderate intensity — walking, swimming, cycling — helps maintain respiratory and cardiovascular fitness. Use supplemental oxygen during exercise if prescribed by the specialist. Avoid exercising in dusty or fume-contaminated environments.
dailyManagement: Avoid re-exposure to tin dust or silica-containing dust in any context, including home renovation work or DIY involving mineral dust.
Monitor breathing daily using a simple breathlessness scale and record any worsening trend to report to the specialist.
Take bronchodilators or other prescribed medications as directed and check inhaler technique at each review.
Seek prompt medical advice for any new haemoptysis, unintentional weight loss, or fever lasting more than 48 hours.
Keep all annual surveillance appointments for chest imaging and pulmonary function tests.
prognosis: Pure stannosis carries an excellent prognosis; radiological opacities persist indefinitely because deposited tin is not cleared from the lung, but lung function remains normal and respiratory symptoms are minimal or absent. Workers who are identified on surveillance and removed from further exposure can expect a stable life course without respiratory disability from the stannosis alone. The prognosis worsens substantially when tin exposure has been mixed with silica: mixed tin-silicosis can cause progressive restriction and respiratory failure over 10-20 years, with risk of tuberculosis superinfection adding further morbidity. All patients should be monitored lifelong for the small but real risk of tuberculosis reactivation and for lung cancer in those with additional tobacco exposure.
recoveryTimeline: There is no reversal of established tin deposition. Radiological opacities persist indefinitely. Following removal from exposure, lung function in pure stannosis stays stable without further decline. In mixed tin-silicosis, progression may slow after cessation of silica exposure but rarely stops completely. Bronchodilator treatment improves symptom burden within days. Pulmonary rehabilitation produces meaningful improvement in exercise capacity after 8-12 weeks of supervised training.
complications: Mixed tin-silicosis — if tin exposure has been accompanied by silica, the silica component drives progressive fibrosis, restriction, and gas-exchange failure over years.
Tuberculosis — all pneumoconioses impair alveolar macrophage killing of Mycobacterium tuberculosis; stannosis patients have elevated TB susceptibility, particularly those in high-incidence countries.
Progressive massive fibrosis — rare in pure stannosis but can occur in mixed disease when smaller fibrotic nodules coalesce into large upper-lobe masses causing severe obstruction and dyspnoea.
Lung cancer — chronic lung inflammation and any co-exposure to carcinogenic compounds in tin mining environments confer a modestly elevated lung cancer risk, particularly in smokers.
Respiratory failure — end-stage fibrotic mixed tin-silicosis can progress to type 1 respiratory failure requiring long-term oxygen therapy.

Psychological impact — the striking appearance of the chest X-ray often causes significant anxiety in patients; reassurance and accurate explanation of the benign nature of pure stannosis are essential parts of management.

supportResources.name: NIOSH — Pneumoconiosis Prevention
supportResources.description: NIOSH guidance on prevention and monitoring of all forms of pneumoconiosis including dust control and health surveillance.

supportResources.name: WHO — Occupational Lung Diseases
supportResources.description: WHO fact sheets and policies on occupational health and dust-related lung disease prevention.

supportResources.name: British Thoracic Society — Occupational Lung Disease
supportResources.description: Clinical guidelines and patient resources on occupational lung diseases from the British Thoracic Society.

sources.title: Weissman DN. Occupational Pulmonary Diseases. In: Murray and Nadel's Textbook of Respiratory Medicine (7th ed), 2022. Elsevier.
NIOSH — Pneumoconiosis: Coal Workers', Silicosis, and Other Dust-Related Lung Diseases
Cowie RL, Mabena SK. Silicosis, occupational mycobacterial disease, and pulmonary tuberculosis in silica-exposed workers. Am Rev Respir Dis, 1991
Greenberg MI, Waksman J, Curtis J. Silicosis: a review. Dis Mon, 2007

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Miliary tuberculosis produces widespread tiny nodules that can mimic stannosis on X-ray, but TB nodules are less radio-opaque than tin, and TB is associated with fever, night sweats, weight loss, and positive TB tests — not with tin dust occupational history.

confusedWithConditions.name: Silicosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Silicosis produces upper-lobe-predominant nodules of lower radiodensity than tin, causes genuine fibrosis and functional decline, and arises from silica exposure; pure stannosis has denser nodules, no fibrosis, and normal lung function.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asbestosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asbestosis causes basilar fibrosis and pleural plaques from asbestos fibre exposure, with progressive restriction; pure stannosis has a completely different nodular pattern without fibrosis and arises in tin rather than asbestos workers.

relatedConditions.name: Silicosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-exposure to silica converts benign stannosis into fibrogenic mixed tin-silicosis, making silicosis the most clinically important related condition.

relatedConditions.name: Asbestosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another benign-to-fibrotic occupational pneumoconiosis caused by inhaled mineral particles; contrasted with stannosis to illustrate the spectrum from benign to progressive disease.

relatedConditions.name: Coal workers' pneumoconiosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Part of the same occupational dust lung disease family; like stannosis, ranges from benign accumulation to serious progressive fibrosis depending on co-exposure to silica.

relatedConditions.name: Berylliosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another rare occupational lung disease from a specific industrial metal; contrasted with stannosis to show that not all metal dust lung diseases are benign — berylliosis causes active immune-mediated damage.

faqs.question: What is stannosis?
faqs.answer: Stannosis is a lung condition caused by inhaling tin oxide dust, mainly in tin mining or smelting work. Tin particles accumulate in lung tissue and appear as very dense, miliary nodules on chest X-rays. Pure stannosis does not cause fibrosis or impair lung function, making it one of the few benign pneumoconioses. Mixed tin and silica exposure can cause more serious lung scarring.

faqs.question: Is stannosis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Pure stannosis — caused by tin oxide alone — is generally benign. It produces striking X-ray changes but does not cause lung fibrosis, and breathing tests remain normal. However, if tin exposure has been combined with silica (common in cassiterite mining), the silica component causes progressive fibrosis, which is a serious condition. All affected workers also have elevated tuberculosis risk that requires monitoring.

faqs.question: Who develops stannosis?
faqs.answer: Stannosis mainly affects workers in cassiterite (tin ore) mining, tin smelting, tin metal refining, and tin compound manufacturing. It is uncommon because the global tin processing workforce is small. Cases have been reported from Bolivia, Malaysia, Indonesia, China, and Australia, and historically from Cornwall in England. Most affected workers are men with careers spanning 15-25 years in the industry.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of stannosis?
faqs.answer: Most people with pure stannosis have no symptoms at all, and the condition is found incidentally on a chest X-ray. When symptoms occur they include mild dry cough, slight breathlessness on exertion, and chest tightness. Significant breathlessness, fever, night sweats, or weight loss are not features of pure stannosis and require urgent investigation for tuberculosis or lung cancer.

faqs.question: Why does stannosis cause such dramatic chest X-ray changes?
faqs.answer: Tin oxide (SnO₂) is exceptionally radio-opaque — roughly three times denser than calcium on X-ray — so even a modest amount of tin deposited in the lungs produces strikingly white, dense nodules that can alarm a clinician unfamiliar with the condition. Despite the alarming appearance, the nodules represent deposited metal rather than fibrosis, and lung function tests are normal in pure disease.

faqs.question: How is stannosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines the occupational history — years working with tin oxide dust — with characteristic chest X-ray findings of dense bilateral nodules and normal pulmonary function tests. HRCT gives more detail. Lung biopsy or bronchoalveolar lavage showing tin-containing macrophages confirms the diagnosis when there is uncertainty. TB and malignancy must be excluded because they can mimic the radiological appearance.

faqs.question: Is stannosis the same as silicosis?
faqs.answer: No. Stannosis is caused by tin oxide and is benign — no fibrosis, preserved lung function. Silicosis is caused by crystalline silica and causes progressive fibrosis and functional decline. However, in cassiterite tin mining, co-exposure to silica is very common, producing mixed tin-silicosis that behaves like silicosis and causes genuine lung damage.

faqs.question: Can stannosis be treated or reversed?
faqs.answer: There is no treatment that removes deposited tin from the lungs. Tin particles remain permanently. Stopping exposure prevents further accumulation and stabilises pure stannosis. Lung function does not decline in pure stannosis after removal from exposure. For symptomatic patients with mixed tin-silicosis or airflow obstruction, bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation, and supplemental oxygen are used as needed.

faqs.question: Does stannosis cause lung fibrosis?
faqs.answer: Pure tin oxide stannosis does not cause fibrosis, which is what makes it benign. Tin oxide does not destroy alveolar macrophages or activate fibroblasts the way silica does. However, when tin ore mining involves drilling through quartz rock, silica co-exposure causes mixed disease with genuine fibrosis. The distinction depends on whether silica was part of the occupational exposure.

faqs.question: Does stannosis increase tuberculosis risk?
faqs.answer: Yes. Like all pneumoconioses, stannosis impairs alveolar macrophage function, making it harder for the lung to contain tuberculosis bacteria. Stannosis patients have an elevated TB risk compared with the general population. All affected workers should have a TB skin test or blood test (IGRA), and those who test positive for latent TB should receive preventive treatment.

faqs.question: What industries cause stannosis?
faqs.answer: Stannosis is caused by exposure to tin oxide dust, primarily in cassiterite (tin ore) mining, tin smelting, tin metal refining, and the manufacturing of inorganic tin compounds. Secondary exposure can occur in foundry work involving tin-containing alloys such as solder, bronze, and pewter. Historically the highest reported rates were in Bolivian tin mines and Cornish tin mines in the UK.

faqs.question: What is the difference between stannosis and siderosis?
faqs.answer: Stannosis is caused by tin oxide and siderosis is caused by iron oxide, but both are benign pneumoconioses that produce striking chest X-ray changes without significant fibrosis. Tin oxide is denser than iron oxide, so stannosis nodules appear even whiter on X-ray. Both conditions are managed primarily by removing the worker from further dust exposure and monitoring lung function annually.

faqs.question: What preventive measures reduce the risk of stannosis?
faqs.answer: Local exhaust ventilation at dust-generating points is the most effective measure. Respirators — N95 or P100 grade — provide supplemental protection. Wet suppression during drilling and crushing reduces airborne dust. Regular health surveillance with chest X-rays and spirometry allows early detection. Eliminating co-exposure to silica in tin mining prevents the much more serious mixed tin-silicosis.

faqs.question: What is the long-term outlook for stannosis?
faqs.answer: Workers with pure stannosis who are removed from further exposure can generally expect stable lung function and minimal respiratory symptoms for the rest of their lives. Radiological nodules persist but do not cause functional deterioration. Those with mixed tin-silicosis face a more variable course, with possible progressive restriction and respiratory failure. Lifelong TB surveillance is recommended for all.

faqs.question: Is stannosis a compensable occupational disease?
faqs.answer: Stannosis is an occupational disease in most jurisdictions, and affected workers may be entitled to compensation. Documenting exposure history with employment records, chest X-rays, and pulmonary function tests supports a compensation claim. An occupational medicine physician can advise on the regulatory framework and the strength of the case in a specific country or jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Should I continue working if I am diagnosed with stannosis?
faqs.answer: After a stannosis diagnosis, you should no longer work in roles with significant tin oxide dust exposure, as continued exposure adds to the lung burden even if symptoms are absent. Many people can continue working in other roles outside the dusty environment. An occupational physician can advise on suitable alternative duties and whether workplace adjustments can reduce exposure sufficiently.

faqs.question: How often should stannosis be monitored?
faqs.answer: Annual follow-up is standard, including chest X-ray and pulmonary function tests (spirometry and DLCO). Any significant change — worsening opacities, declining lung function, new symptoms — warrants more frequent review and possibly HRCT. TB testing should be performed at diagnosis and repeated if high-risk exposures occur. Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination should be kept up to date.

faqs.question: Does stannosis affect life expectancy?
faqs.answer: Pure stannosis without associated fibrosis or complicating disease does not shorten life expectancy. The outlook is very similar to that of an unaffected person of the same age. Life expectancy is shortened when mixed tin-silicosis develops with progressive fibrosis, when TB supervenes, or when lung cancer develops — risks that increase with smoking and silica co-exposure.

faqs.question: Can stannosis develop from brief tin exposure?
faqs.answer: Stannosis generally requires years of substantial occupational exposure to tin oxide dust to produce visible radiological changes — typically 15-25 years in significant dust environments. Brief or low-level exposure is unlikely to cause detectable stannosis. The condition is therefore almost always found in career tin workers rather than in those with incidental or very limited contact.

faqs.question: Is stannosis related to tin poisoning?
faqs.answer: No. Stannosis is not tin poisoning. Tin itself is a relatively non-toxic element, and the condition is caused by physical particle accumulation rather than toxic chemical effects. Tin is not fibrogenic or carcinogenic. The radiological changes reflect the physical density and persistence of deposited tin particles, not organ damage from tin toxicity.

metaDescription: Stannosis is a benign occupational lung disease from tin oxide inhalation in miners and smelters. Learn causes, X-ray findings, and management options.
